section.title: War with the Ammonites
section.sectionRationale: David tries to show kindness to the new Ammonite king Hanun, but Hanun, misled by his advisors, humiliates David's envoys and provokes war. The Ammonites hire Aramean armies, and Joab, caught between two forces, calls Israel to fight bravely and trust the LORD to do what is good. Israel routs both Ammon and Aram. The movement sets the military stage for chapter 11, since it is during this very Ammonite war that David stays home and falls into sin with Bathsheba.
section.thesis: David's genuine kindness is met with suspicion and insult, dragging Israel into war; yet in the face of encircling enemies Joab rallies the army to courage and to trust that the LORD will do what is good in His sight, and God grants a sweeping victory, setting the stage for David's own failure while he lingers at home.
section.frameNote: Three beats: David's kindness to Hanun repaid with the disgrace of his envoys, verses 1 to 5; the Ammonites hire the Arameans and Joab faces them on two fronts with a call to courage and trust, verses 6 to 14; and the decisive defeat of the regrouped Arameans, verses 15 to 19.

verses.text: Some time later, the king of the Ammonites died and was succeeded by his son Hanun.

And David said, “I will show {{chesed|kindness}} to Hanun son of Nahash, just as his father showed kindness to me.” So David sent some of his servants to console Hanun concerning his father. But when they arrived in the land of the Ammonites,

the princes of the Ammonites said to Hanun their lord, “Just because David has sent you comforters, do you really believe he is showing respect for your father? Has not David instead sent his servants to explore the city, spy it out, and overthrow it?”
So Hanun took David’s servants, shaved off half of each man’s beard, cut off their garments at the hips, and sent them away.

When this was reported to David, he sent messengers to meet the men, since they had been thoroughly humiliated. The king told them, “Stay in Jericho until your beards have grown back, and then return.”

units.label: Battle on two fronts
units.note: may the LORD do what is good

verses.text: When the Ammonites realized that they had become a stench to David, they hired twenty thousand Aramean foot soldiers from Beth-rehob and Zoba, as well as a thousand men from the king of Maacah and twelve thousand men from Tob.
On hearing this, David sent Joab and the entire army of mighty men.

The Ammonites marched out and arrayed themselves for battle at the entrance of the city gate, while the Arameans of Zobah and Rehob and the men of Tob and Maacah were by themselves in the open country.
When Joab saw the battle lines before him and behind him, he selected some of the best men of Israel and arrayed them against the Arameans.
And he placed the rest of the troops under the command of his brother Abishai, who arrayed them against the Ammonites.
“If the Arameans are too strong for me,” said Joab, “then you will come to my rescue. And if the Ammonites are too strong for you, then I will come to your rescue.
Be {{chazaq|strong}} and let us fight bravely for our people and for the cities of our God. May the LORD do what is good in His sight.”
So Joab and his troops advanced to fight the Arameans, who fled before him.
When the Ammonites saw that the Arameans had fled, they too fled before Abishai, and they entered the city. So Joab returned from fighting against the Ammonites and came to Jerusalem.
When the Arameans saw that they had been defeated by Israel, they regrouped.
Hadadezer sent messengers to bring more Arameans from beyond the Euphrates, and they came to Helam with Shobach the commander of Hadadezer’s army leading them.
When this was reported to David, he gathered all Israel, crossed the Jordan, and went to Helam. Then the Arameans arrayed themselves against David and fought against him.
But the Arameans fled before Israel, and David killed seven hundred charioteers and forty thousand foot soldiers. He also struck down Shobach the commander of their army, who died there.

When all the kings who were subject to Hadadezer saw that they had been defeated by Israel, they made peace with Israel and became subject to them. So the Arameans were afraid to help the Ammonites anymore.

chesed.gloss: Loving devotion, steadfast kindness, loyalty. David seeks to show chesed to Hanun as Hanun's father Nahash had shown to David, extending covenant kindness even beyond Israel. That his good faith is met with suspicion and insult sets the tragic tone of the chapter, and shows that offered kindness is not always received.

chazaq.gloss: To be strong, firm, courageous, to take hold and hold fast. Joab's charge, 'be strong and let us fight bravely,' calls the troops to steady courage, not in their own might but coupled with trust that the LORD will do what is good in His sight. The same word runs through the calls to courage given to Joshua and to God's people facing daunting odds.

basic.context: David, having shown kindness to Mephibosheth, now tries to extend kindness beyond Israel's borders. When the Ammonite king Nahash dies, David sends envoys to comfort his son Hanun, remembering some past kindness Nahash had shown him. But Hanun's advisors persuade him that the envoys are really spies sent to scout the city for conquest. So Hanun grossly insults them, shaving off half of each man's beard and cutting their robes off at the hips, and sends them away shamed. Realizing they have deeply offended David, the Ammonites hire large Aramean mercenary armies. David sends Joab and the army. Joab finds himself trapped between the Ammonites at the city gate and the Arameans in the open field, so he splits his forces, takes the Arameans himself, gives the Ammonites to his brother Abishai, and rallies everyone with a memorable charge: be strong, fight bravely for our people and the cities of our God, and may the LORD do what is good in His sight. Both enemies flee. When the Arameans regroup with reinforcements, David himself leads Israel and crushes them, so that Aram makes peace and will no longer help Ammon. The chapter raises the questions of how good intentions can spiral into war, where true courage comes from, and, ominously, what David will do next, for it is during this Ammonite siege that chapter 11 unfolds.
basic.hermeneutics: This is historical narrative reporting a diplomatic insult and the war it triggered, teaching by the flow of events rather than by direct command. It shows that offered kindness is sometimes met with suspicion and cruelty, and that leaders may be dragged into conflict they did not seek. Joab's charge in verse 12 is a model of holding together human responsibility, be strong and fight bravely, with humble trust in God's sovereign will, may the LORD do what is good. The chapter is also carefully placed to set up David's fall: the war provides the very occasion for his sin in chapter 11.
basic.originalLanguages: The chapter opens with David wanting to show chesed, loving devotion, to Hanun as his father had shown David, kindness offered and then thrown back in insult. At its center is Joab's charge to chazaq, to be strong and courageous, a courage that is not bravado but is joined to trust that the LORD will do what is good in His sight. The two words frame the movement: kindness rejected leads to war, and true strength in war rests on trusting God's good will.
basic.typology: Joab's summons to be strong and to fight for the people and the cities of God, while leaving the outcome to the LORD, models the posture of God's people in every battle: wholehearted effort joined to trust in God's sovereign goodness. This anticipates the Christian's call to stand firm and contend for what is good while resting the result in the hands of God, who works all things according to His will.
basic.christ: David seeks to show kindness and is met with hostility and insult, a small foretaste of the far greater rejection Christ would face, whose kindness to the world was answered with contempt and a cross. And the confidence that the LORD will do what is good in His sight points to Christ, who in Gethsemane entrusted Himself to the Father's good will, praying not my will but Yours be done, and won the decisive victory through that trust.
basic.god: God is the one to whose will Joab defers, the LORD who does what is good in His sight, so that even the tangled affairs of insulted envoys and hired armies unfold under His sovereign hand. God grants Israel victory over overwhelming odds, showing Himself faithful to His people. Yet the chapter also quietly reminds us that God's good purposes advance even as human sin, soon David's own, works its damage within them.
basic.crossReferences: Deuteronomy 31 and Joshua 1 give the great calls to be strong and courageous that Joab's charge echoes. First Chronicles 19 is the parallel account of this war. And Christ's trust in the Father's will in Gethsemane, Luke 22, fulfills the spirit of Joab's may the LORD do what is good.
basic.oneStory: David's offered kindness is spurned, Israel is drawn into war, and God grants victory as His people fight bravely and trust His good will. The chapter advances the story to the edge of David's great fall, for this Ammonite war is the very setting of his sin, and it sets before us a trust in God's sovereign goodness that finds its perfection in Christ, who entrusted Himself wholly to the Father's will.

context.literary: This war chapter serves as the frame for the Bathsheba narrative that follows: chapter 11 opens 'in the spring, when kings go off to war,' referring to this same Ammonite campaign, during which David stays in Jerusalem. Thus chapter 10 is not a detour but the setting of the crisis. It also rounds off the account of David's wars begun in chapter 8. Joab's battlefield speech is the theological high point, holding courage and trust together.
context.historical: Shaving half a beard and cutting off garments at the buttocks were calculated humiliations in a culture where the beard signified honor and manhood; the insult to royal envoys was tantamount to a declaration of war. The Ammonites' hiring of Aramean mercenaries reflects the coalition politics of the region. Jericho, near the Jordan, was a discreet place for the shamed envoys to wait. The scale of the Aramean forces shows the seriousness of the threat David's kingdom had provoked.
context.canonical: The chapter continues the narrative of David's God-given victories while functioning as the doorway to the book's turning point. Joab's charge echoes the Torah's repeated 'be strong and courageous' and anticipates the New Testament call to stand firm. Placed just before chapter 11, it heightens the tragedy: at the very moment Israel's army is fighting bravely for the cities of God, its king will be at home falling into grievous sin.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To show how David's kindness was spurned and turned to war, to model in Joab's charge a courage joined to trust in the LORD's good will, and to record God's victory over Ammon and Aram, all while setting the stage for David's fall in the very war here begun.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The chapter describes a historical insult and war rather than prescribing military action; it does not command God's people to answer insult with battle. What endures is the recognition that offered kindness may be rejected, the model of wholehearted effort joined to humble trust in God's sovereign goodness, and the sober awareness that seasons of victory can be seasons of spiritual danger.

debates.question: Was David's mission to Hanun a sincere kindness or a political maneuver?

views.label: A sincere act of covenant kindness
views.case: The narrator presents David's motive plainly as chesed, showing kindness because Hanun's father had shown kindness to him, verse 2; this fits David's established pattern of loyalty, as with Mephibosheth just before, and the tragedy of the chapter depends on the mission being genuine, so that Hanun's suspicion is a cruel misreading of real good faith.

views.label: A goodwill gesture with a diplomatic dimension
views.case: Some note that condolence embassies between kings also carried political weight, cementing alliances, so David's kindness, while sincere, was not naive of statecraft; even so, the text frames it as kindness repaid with insult, and nothing suggests the envoys were actually spies as Hanun's advisors feared.

debates.bearing: Both agree the envoys were not spies and that David acted in good faith; the chapter's point is that genuine kindness was met with suspicion and humiliation, a reminder that offered grace is not always received, and a foreshadowing of the far greater kindness of Christ that the world would reject.

study.originalLanguages: The movement is framed by two Hebrew words. It opens with David resolving to show chesed, loving devotion, to Hanun as Hanun's father had shown chesed to David, a covenant kindness reaching even beyond Israel, only to be thrown back in insult and war. At its heart stands Joab's charge to chazaq, to be strong and courageous: 'Be strong and let us fight bravely for our people and for the cities of our God. May the LORD do what is good in His sight,' verse 12. This chazaq is no mere bravado; it is courage yoked to trust, wholehearted human effort offered up while the outcome is left entirely to the LORD's good will. The pairing captures the chapter's spiritual center, that God's people are to act with all their strength and yet rest the result in the hands of the God who does what is good, a posture perfected in Christ's own 'not My will but Yours' in Gethsemane.

typology.type: Kindness offered and met with rejection and insult
typology.fulfillment: Christ, whose kindness to the world was answered with contempt and crucifixion, John 1:11.

typology.type: Courage joined to 'may the LORD do what is good in His sight'
typology.fulfillment: Christ entrusting Himself to the Father's will in Gethsemane, 'not My will but Yours be done,' Luke 22:42.

typology.type: Fighting for the people and the cities of God
typology.fulfillment: The church's call to stand firm and contend for the faith while resting in God's sovereign purpose, 1 Corinthians 16:13 and Jude 3.

study.christ: David seeks to show kindness and is answered with suspicion, humiliation, and war, a small foreshadowing of the far greater rejection of Christ, who came to His own in kindness and was met with contempt and a cross, John 1:11. And the confidence at the heart of the chapter, that the LORD will do what is good in His sight, points to Christ, who in Gethsemane entrusted Himself wholly to the Father's good will, praying 'not My will but Yours be done,' Luke 22:42, and who won the decisive victory precisely through that trust. Joab's charge to be strong and fight for the people and the cities of God, leaving the outcome to the LORD, is taken up by the church that contends for the faith while resting in the sovereign goodness of God fulfilled in Christ.
study.god: God is the one to whose sovereign will Joab defers, the LORD who does what is good in His sight, so that even the tangled affairs of insulted envoys, hired mercenaries, and battles on two fronts unfold under His governing hand. He grants Israel victory over daunting odds, proving Himself faithful to defend His people and their cities. And yet the chapter, by its very placement, quietly reminds us that God's good purposes move forward even as human sin, soon David's own adultery and murder, works its damage within the same season of war, so that God's goodness and human failure are held together in the unfolding story.

crossReferences.note: The LORD's repeated charge to Joshua to be strong and courageous, echoed in Joab's battlefield summons.
The parallel account of the Ammonite and Aramean war, confirming and supplementing this chapter.
Christ's 'not My will but Yours be done,' the perfection of Joab's 'may the LORD do what is good in His sight.'
The next chapter opens in this same Ammonite war, when David remains in Jerusalem and falls into sin, showing chapter 10 as the setting of the crisis.

study.oneStory: David's offered kindness is spurned, Israel is dragged into war, and God grants sweeping victory as His people fight bravely and entrust the outcome to His good will. The chapter carries the story to the very brink of David's great fall, for this Ammonite war is the setting of his sin with Bathsheba, and it holds before us a trust in God's sovereign goodness, 'may the LORD do what is good in His sight,' that finds its perfection in Jesus, who entrusted Himself wholly to the Father's will and through that trust won the victory for His people.

quiz.q: Why does David send envoys to the Ammonite king Hanun?

options.text: To show kindness and console him, because Hanun's father Nahash had shown kindness to David.
options.note: Verse 2 states David's motive of kindness in return for kindness received.

options.text: To spy out the Ammonite city in preparation for conquest.
options.note: That is Hanun's advisors' false suspicion; David's actual purpose was kindness.

options.text: To offer condolences to Hanun on his father's death.
options.note: True, but this option omits that David acted to repay the kindness Nahash had shown him.

options.text: To demand tribute from the new Ammonite king.
options.note: David sends comfort, not a demand for tribute; the mission is one of kindness.

quiz.q: How does Hanun treat David's envoys?

options.text: He grossly humiliates them, shaving off half of each man's beard and cutting off their garments, then sends them away.
options.note: Verse 4 records the calculated insult that provokes war.

options.text: He welcomes them warmly and sends them home with gifts.
options.note: The opposite; he insults and shames them rather than welcoming them.

options.text: He shames the envoys and sends them back disgraced.
options.note: True, but this option omits the specific, deliberate humiliations of the shaved beards and cut garments.

options.text: He imprisons them as suspected spies.
options.note: He does not imprison them; he humiliates them and sends them away, an act tantamount to declaring war.

quiz.q: What is the heart of Joab's charge to the army as they face two enemies?

options.text: Be strong and fight bravely for our people and the cities of our God, and may the LORD do what is good in His sight.
options.note: Verse 12 joins wholehearted courage to trust in the LORD's sovereign will.

options.text: Fight to the last man, for defeat is unthinkable and retreat forbidden.
options.note: Joab calls for courage but explicitly leaves the outcome to the LORD, not a vow of no retreat.

options.text: Be courageous and fight for God's people, trusting the outcome to the LORD.
options.note: True, but this option softens the memorable words 'may the LORD do what is good in His sight' that anchor his trust.

options.text: Trust in our superior numbers and training to carry the day.
options.note: Israel was outnumbered and encircled; Joab points to courage and the LORD's will, not superior forces.

quiz.q: Why is this war chapter placed just before chapter 11?

options.text: Because it is during this very Ammonite war that David stays home and falls into sin with Bathsheba.
options.note: 2 Samuel 11:1 opens in this same campaign, making chapter 10 the setting of the crisis.

options.text: Because it simply finishes the list of David's wars with no connection to what follows.
options.note: The chapter is deliberately the setting for David's fall, not an unrelated appendix.

options.text: Because it provides the military backdrop against which David's failure unfolds.
options.note: True, but this option omits the striking irony that the army fights bravely for God's cities while the king sins at home.

options.text: Because it explains why David could no longer trust his commander Joab.
options.note: The chapter does not concern a breakdown of trust with Joab; it sets the stage for the Bathsheba narrative.

quiz.q: How does the chapter point toward Christ?

options.text: David's spurned kindness foreshadows Christ's rejection, and 'may the LORD do what is good' anticipates Christ's trust in the Father's will in Gethsemane.
options.note: Both the rejected kindness and the deference to God's will point to Christ, John 1:11 and Luke 22:42.

options.text: It teaches that God's people should answer every insult with military force.
options.note: The chapter is descriptive of ancient war, not a command to answer insult with battle, and Christ's way is otherwise.

options.text: It models trusting God's sovereign goodness with the outcome of our efforts.
options.note: True, but this option omits the way David's rejected kindness foreshadows the rejection of Christ.

options.text: It proves that God always gives victory to the side with the best strategy.
options.note: Israel was outnumbered; the chapter credits the LORD's good will, not superior strategy, for the victory.

questions: David's genuine kindness was met with suspicion and insult. Have you ever had your good intentions misread, and how did you respond when kindness was thrown back at you?
Hanun trusted cynical advisors who assumed the worst about David. Whose voices are shaping how you interpret others' motives, and are they leading you toward suspicion or trust?
Joab said, 'Be strong and let us fight bravely, and may the LORD do what is good in His sight.' Where do you need to give your wholehearted effort while genuinely leaving the outcome to God?
Israel's army was fighting bravely for the cities of God at the very time their king was about to fall into sin at home. How can times of outward success mask spiritual danger in your own life?
Christ entrusted Himself to the Father's will in Gethsemane. How does His 'not My will but Yours' shape the way you pray about outcomes you cannot control?

translationNotes.note: Shaving half the beard and cutting the garments at the hips were deliberate, shameful humiliations; in that culture the beard signified honor, so insulting royal envoys this way amounted to a declaration of war.
The BSB footnote notes that 'beyond the Euphrates' renders the Hebrew 'the River,' the Euphrates, from which Hadadezer summoned Aramean reinforcements.

The BSB footnote notes that 'foot soldiers' follows some Septuagint manuscripts and 1 Chronicles 19:18, while the Hebrew reads 'horsemen'; such numerical and detail variants are common between Samuel and Chronicles.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for 2 Samuel 10:1-19. No em dashes or en dashes appear in the source text for this chapter; none introduced in this file.
